items.Name: Multi-Spectral Camera
items.Description: The multi-spectral camera took detailed images of the asteroid Eros. It helped scientists study the surface shape, craters, and rock types. These images gave insight into how Eros formed and changed over time.

items.Name: X-Ray/Gamma-Ray Spectrometer
items.Description: This instrument measured the elements on Eros’ surface. It helped determine the asteroid’s mineral makeup. The data showed what materials were present and improved understanding of early solar system bodies.

items.Name: Sojourner Rover
items.Description: Sojourner was the first rover to operate beyond the Earth-Moon system. It explored the Martian surface and tested new technology for future missions. The rover proved that low-cost robotic exploration of Mars was possible.

items.Name: Radiation-Hardened IBM RISC 6000 Computer
items.Description: The multi-spectral camera took detailed images of the asteroid Eros. It helped scientists study the surface shape, craters, and rock types. These images gave insight into how Eros formed and changed over time.

items.Name: Gamma Ray Spectrometer
items.Description: This onboard computer controlled the lander and rover. It was specially designed to survive harsh space radiation. The computer processed data and sent important information back to Earth.

items.Name: Neutron Spectrometer
items.Description: The neutron spectrometer searched for hydrogen near the lunar poles. Its findings suggested the presence of water ice. This discovery was important for future human exploration.

items.Name: Aerogel Dust Collector
items.Description: The aerogel collector captured tiny dust particles from Comet Wild 2. It gently slowed the particles without damaging them. These samples were returned to Earth for detailed study.

items.Name: Sample Return Capsule
items.Description: This capsule brought comet and cosmic dust samples back to Earth. It safely re-entered the atmosphere and landed in Utah. It marked the first U.S. mission to return samples from a comet.

items.Name: Genesis Collectors
items.Description: These collectors captured particles from the solar wind. They allowed scientists to study the Sun’s composition directly. This helped improve models of how the solar system formed.

items.Name: Drogue Parachute
items.Description: The drogue parachute was designed to slow the return capsule during reentry. Although the main parachutes failed, this system was part of the recovery plan. The returned material was still partially recovered and studied.

items.Name: Mercury Dual Imaging System (MDIS)
items.Description: MDIS took detailed images of Mercury’s surface. It mapped craters, cliffs, and volcanic features. The images transformed understanding of Mercury’s geology.

items.Name: Bipropellant Large Velocity Assist Thruster
items.Description: This powerful thruster helped the spacecraft adjust speed and enter Mercury’s orbit. It used hydrazine and nitrogen tetroxide. The engine made it possible to become the first spacecraft to orbit Mercury.

items.Name: Copper-Core Smart Impactor
items.Description: The impactor crashed into Comet Tempel 1. It released material from beneath the comet’s surface. Scientists studied the debris to learn what the nucleus was made of.

items.Name: High Resolution Imager (HRI)
items.Description: The HRI captured detailed images of the comet before and after impact. It helped analyze the crater and ejected material. These images gave insight into comet structure.

items.Name: Ion Propulsion System
items.Description: Dawn’s ion engines used xenon gas to create gentle but efficient thrust. This system allowed the spacecraft to orbit both Vesta and Ceres. It made Dawn the first mission to visit two protoplanets.

items.Name: Visible and Infrared Spectrometer (VIR)
items.Description: VIR studied the surface minerals of Vesta and Ceres. It detected signs of water-related materials on Ceres. This helped scientists understand early planetary formation.

items.Name: Photometer
items.Description: Kepler’s photometer measured tiny changes in starlight brightness. These changes revealed planets passing in front of stars. It led to the discovery of thousands of exoplanets.

items.Name: Schmidt Camera
items.Description: The Schmidt camera design allowed Kepler to monitor a wide field of stars at once. It focused light precisely onto the photometer. This design made long-term planet hunting possible.

items.Name: Ebb
items.Description: Ebb was one of the twin spacecraft that orbited the Moon. It worked with Flow to measure tiny changes in distance between them. These measurements revealed details about the Moon’s gravity field.

items.Name: Ka-Band Lunar Gravity Ranging System (LGRS)
items.Description: LGRS measured the distance between the two spacecraft very precisely. It detected small variations caused by changes in lunar gravity. This helped map the Moon’s interior structure.

items.Name: Seismic Experiment for Interior Structure (SEIS)
items.Description: SEIS measured marsquakes and vibrations in the Martian ground. It provided the first detailed look at Mars’ interior layers. This helped scientists understand how rocky planets form.

items.Name: Heat Flow and Physical Properties Package (HP³)
items.Description: This instrument was designed to measure heat coming from inside Mars. It aimed to show how much energy the planet still produces. Although it faced technical challenges, it provided valuable engineering data.

items.Name: High-Resolution Visible Imager (L’LORRI)
items.Description: L’LORRI captures detailed images of Jupiter’s Trojan asteroids. It helps map their shapes and surface features. These images reveal clues about early solar system materials.

items.Name: Optical and Near-Infrared Imaging Spectrometer (L’Ralph)
items.Description: L’Ralph studies asteroid surface composition. It detects minerals and organic materials. This helps scientists compare Trojan asteroids to other small bodies.

items.Name: Psyche Multispectral Imager
items.Description: This camera system will map the surface of asteroid 16 Psyche. It will look for differences in metal and rock composition. The images will help determine if the asteroid is an exposed planetary core.

items.Name: Deep Space Optical Communications (DSOC)
items.Description: DSOC tests laser communication in deep space. It aims to send data much faster than traditional radio systems. This technology could improve future missions across the solar system.
